completeness.rationale: Captured nearly all expected information: intermittent ~1-in-3 rate, 2-day onset, analyst role correlation, admin unaffected, multiple users affected. The load-balanced environment was correctly inferred rather than confirmed by the reporter. Editor/viewer roles were not explicitly checked. Minor gaps: exact role assignment date vs. deployment date correlation not fully pinned.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis precisely matches both acceptable diagnostic paths: the agent correctly identified the ~1-in-3 pattern as indicative of a load-balanced multi-server setup with one stale instance, AND correctly connected the analyst role assignment to the onset of the issue. The hypothesis is essentially the ground truth stated at the infrastructure level without having direct access to server configs.

thoroughness.rationale: Asked all critical questions to establish role-specificity, pattern regularity, and multi-user scope. The one obvious skip was asking directly whether a deployment or configuration change occurred ~2 days ago — this was listed as an information gap rather than pursued. Given the strong hypothesis already formed, resolving without it was reasonable but not ideal.

economy.rationale: All three question turns were essential. Q1 scoped the failure mode. Q2 precisely targeted the analyst role after the reporter volunteered the detail. Q3 validated the regularity of the pattern to support the load balancer inference. No redundant or low-value questions.

question_quality.rationale: Q2 was particularly sharp — the agent immediately picked up on 'just got the analyst role' and turned it into a direct diagnostic probe about role correlation and whether other roles were affected. Q3 was equally insightful, asking specifically about pattern consistency to distinguish load balancer round-robin from random transient errors. Both questions drew out information the reporter would not have volunteered.

actionability.rationale: The summary is exceptionally actionable: it includes a precise root cause hypothesis, numbered reproduction steps, a recommended fix with specific investigative steps, a proposed test case for verifying the fix, and explicit information gaps for follow-up. A developer could begin checking server configs and deployment logs immediately.

notable_strengths: Immediately recognized the analyst role detail in the reporter's first response and built the next question around it
Correctly inferred a 3-server load balancer topology purely from the reporter's description of the ~every-third-refresh failure pattern
Final summary is production-quality: includes reproduction steps, severity, impact, workaround implications, and a proposed test case
notable_weaknesses: Did not ask directly whether a deployment or config change occurred ~2 days ago — this would have been the highest-value confirmatory question and is listed only as an information gap
Did not check whether editor or viewer roles are also unaffected, leaving the role-specificity claim slightly underconfirmed
most_insightful_question: The third question — asking whether the roughly-every-third-refresh pattern was consistent and whether immediate re-refresh restores access — was the key diagnostic move. The confirmation of a regular, recoverable failure pattern is what validated the load balancer round-robin hypothesis over transient network errors.
missed_opportunities: Ask whether any deployment, configuration update, or infrastructure change occurred around the same time the analyst role was rolled out
Ask whether the 403 appears on other analyst-accessible pages or only the dashboard, to scope whether this is a global RBAC issue or dashboard-specific
